name: Colorectal cancer screening
screensFor: Colorectal (colon and rectum) cancer and precancerous polyps
frequency: Every 10 years (colonoscopy); or stool-based every 1-3 years
test: Colonoscopy every 10 yrs; OR FIT/gFOBT annually; OR FIT-DNA (Cologuard) every 1-3 yrs; OR CT colonography or flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 yrs
guideline: USPSTF 2021 (45-49 Grade B, 50-75 Grade A); ACS 2018
note: Screening starts at 45 (lowered from 50 in the 2021 USPSTF update; ACS since 2018). Grade A for 50-75, Grade B for 45-49. For adults 76-85, screen selectively based on health and prior screening (USPSTF Grade C). Any abnormal non-colonoscopy test must be followed by colonoscopy.

name: Colorectal cancer screening (family history / high risk)
screensFor: Colorectal cancer in people at increased risk
frequency: Colonoscopy every 5 years (or earlier/more often per risk)
test: Colonoscopy, typically starting at age 40 or 10 years before the age of the youngest affected relative, whichever is earlier
guideline: US Multi-Society Task Force on Colorectal Cancer 2017; ACG 2021
note: For those with a first-degree relative diagnosed with colorectal cancer or advanced adenoma. Start at 40, or 10 years before the relative's age at diagnosis. People with inherited syndromes (Lynch, FAP) or IBD need specialized, earlier and more frequent screening. Colonoscopy is the preferred modality for high-risk individuals.

name: Breast cancer screening (mammography)
frequency: Every 2 years (biennial)
test: Screening mammography (digital or digital breast tomosynthesis/3D)
note: USPSTF 2024 update: all women should begin biennial screening at age 40 (previously starting in the 40s was individualized). Continue through age 74. Evidence is insufficient for women 75+, and for supplemental ultrasound/MRI in dense breasts. ACS offers annual option from 45 (optional from 40). Applies to women at average risk.

name: Breast cancer screening (high risk / family history)
frequency: Annual mammography plus annual breast MRI
test: Annual mammography AND annual breast MRI; consider genetic counseling/BRCA testing
guideline: ACS 2007 (high-risk MRI); USPSTF 2019 BRCA risk assessment (Grade B)
note: For women with a lifetime risk ~20% or greater (e.g., known BRCA1/2 or other pathogenic variant, first-degree relative with such a mutation, prior chest radiation age 10-30, or certain syndromes). ACS recommends annual MRI + mammography starting at age 30. USPSTF recommends risk assessment / genetic counseling and BRCA testing for women with a family history suggestive of BRCA mutations.

name: Cervical cancer screening (Pap/HPV)
screensFor: Cervical cancer and high-grade precancerous lesions
frequency: Every 3 years (cytology) or every 5 years (HPV/co-test)
test: Ages 21-29: Pap (cytology) every 3 yrs. Ages 30-65: high-risk HPV test every 5 yrs, OR co-test (HPV + Pap) every 5 yrs, OR Pap alone every 3 yrs
note: Screen ages 21-65. Do not screen before 21 or after 65 (with adequate prior negative screening and not high risk). ACS 2020 prefers starting at 25 with primary HPV testing every 5 years. Women without a cervix (total hysterectomy for benign disease) and no history of high-grade lesions do not need screening. Applies to anyone with a cervix.

name: Lung cancer screening (LDCT)
screensFor: Lung cancer in people with significant smoking history
frequency: Annually
test: Low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) of the chest
note: Annual LDCT for adults 50-80 with a 20+ pack-year history who currently smoke OR quit within the past 15 years. USPSTF 2021 lowered the start age from 55 to 50 and pack-years from 30 to 20. ACS 2023 removed the years-since-quit requirement. Stop after 15 years smoke-free or if a condition limits life expectancy. Requires shared decision-making and a cessation offer.

name: Prostate cancer screening (PSA, shared decision)
frequency: Individualized (commonly every 1-2 years) after shared decision-making
test: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) blood test, with or without digital rectal exam
note: For men 55-69, the decision to screen with PSA should be individual, made after discussing benefits and harms with a clinician. USPSTF recommends AGAINST PSA screening for men 70 and older. ACS recommends the shared-decision discussion begin at 50 for average risk, 45 for higher risk, and 40 for highest risk.

name: Prostate cancer screening (higher risk)
frequency: Individualized after shared decision-making
test: PSA blood test (+/- digital rectal exam)
note: ACS recommends starting the PSA shared-decision-making conversation earlier for higher-risk men: age 45 for Black men and men with a first-degree relative diagnosed before age 65; age 40 for men with multiple first-degree relatives affected at an early age.

name: Skin cancer awareness / examination
frequency: No fixed interval; ongoing self-awareness, clinician exam as indicated
test: Visual skin examination by a clinician; skin self-awareness/self-exam (ABCDE rule for moles)
note: USPSTF concludes evidence is insufficient to recommend for or against routine whole-body skin cancer screening in asymptomatic adults. ACS advises awareness of new or changing skin lesions using the ABCDE warning signs. High-risk people (many/atypical moles, prior skin cancer, strong family history, heavy sun exposure, fair skin) should discuss regular dermatologic exams.

name: Blood pressure screening (hypertension)
screensFor: High blood pressure, a leading modifiable risk factor for heart disease, stroke, and kidney disease
frequency: 18-39 with normal BP: every 3-5 years. Adults 40+ or at increased risk: annually
test: Office blood pressure measurement, confirmed with out-of-office (ambulatory or home BP) monitoring before starting treatment
note: USPSTF recommends screening all adults 18+. Confirmation with ambulatory or home BP monitoring is advised before diagnosis. People with elevated readings, obesity, or African American ancestry should be screened annually.

name: Lipid panel (cholesterol) screening
screensFor: Dyslipidemia (high LDL, low HDL, high triglycerides) to estimate cardiovascular disease risk
frequency: Every 4-6 years for low-risk adults; more often if borderline, on treatment, or additional risk factors
test: Fasting or non-fasting lipid panel; results feed a 10-year ASCVD risk estimate; consider coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring in intermediate-risk adults
guideline: ACC/AHA 2018 Cholesterol; ACC/AHA 2019 Primary Prevention; USPSTF 2016
note: ACC/AHA recommends a first lipid measurement in all adults starting at age 20 and repeat risk assessment every 4-6 years for ages 40-75. Start earlier for those with diabetes, hypertension, obesity, smoking, chronic kidney disease, or a family history of premature heart disease or familial hypercholesterolemia.

name: Type 2 diabetes and prediabetes screening
screensFor: Prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (abnormal blood glucose regulation)
frequency: At least every 3 years if normal; more often with prediabetes or added risk factors
test: Fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, or oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)
guideline: USPSTF 2021 (Grade B); ADA Standards of Care 2025
note: USPSTF recommends screening adults 35-70 who are overweight or obese (BMI >=25, or >=23 in Asian Americans). ADA is broader: universal screening for all adults from 35 regardless of weight, and at any age for overweight adults with a risk factor (family history, hypertension, dyslipidemia, inactivity, high-risk ethnicity, prior gestational diabetes, PCOS).

name: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) screening
screensFor: Abdominal aortic aneurysm, a potentially fatal enlargement of the aorta
frequency: One-time ultrasound
test: One-time abdominal duplex ultrasonography
note: USPSTF recommends a one-time screening ultrasound for men 65-75 who have ever smoked. For men 65-75 who never smoked, selectively offer. Recommends against routine screening in women who never smoked and have no family history.

name: Osteoporosis screening (women 65+)
screensFor: Osteoporosis / low bone mineral density to prevent fragility fractures
frequency: At least once; re-screening interval individualized
test: Central DXA bone mineral density of hip and lumbar spine, with or without clinical fracture risk assessment
note: Applies to all women 65 and older regardless of risk factors (final recommendation Jan 2025). Optimal re-screening interval is uncertain. Not recommended for men (insufficient evidence).

name: Osteoporosis screening (postmenopausal women under 65 at risk)
screensFor: Osteoporosis / low bone mineral density to prevent fragility fractures
frequency: Once increased risk is identified; interval individualized
test: Apply a clinical risk tool (e.g., FRAX, OST) after finding a risk factor; if elevated risk, confirm with central DXA
note: For postmenopausal women younger than 65 at increased fracture risk. Risk factors include low body weight, parental hip fracture, smoking, excess alcohol, prior fracture, rheumatoid arthritis, and long-term glucocorticoid use.

name: Comprehensive eye examination
screensFor: Refractive error, glaucoma, cataract, macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy
frequency: Baseline at 40; 40-54 every 2-4 yrs; 55-64 every 1-3 yrs; 65+ every 1-2 yrs
test: Dilated comprehensive medical eye evaluation by an ophthalmologist or optometrist
note: Frequencies are for asymptomatic adults without risk factors. People with diabetes, hypertension, family history of eye disease, or higher glaucoma risk need earlier and more frequent exams.

name: Hearing loss screening (older adults)
frequency: Periodically; ask about and evaluate hearing changes
test: Single-question hearing inquiry, whispered-voice or finger-rub test, or questionnaire; refer for audiometry if concern
note: USPSTF found insufficient evidence to recommend for or against routine screening of asymptomatic adults 50+. Clinicians should still ask about hearing and evaluate anyone reporting difficulty.

name: Routine dental examination and cleaning
screensFor: Dental caries, gum disease, oral cancer, and other oral health problems
frequency: Typically every 6 months; tailored by dentist to risk
test: Clinical oral examination, professional cleaning, periodic radiographs, oral cancer screening
note: ADA recommends the visit frequency be personalized by the dentist based on oral health rather than a universal fixed interval. Low-risk patients may need only annual visits; high-risk patients every 3-4 months.

name: Obesity screening (BMI)
screensFor: Obesity (to offer intensive behavioral / weight-loss interventions)
frequency: Measure at routine visits (typically annually)
test: Height and weight to calculate body mass index (BMI); BMI 30+ defines obesity in adults
note: USPSTF recommends screening all adults for obesity and offering or referring adults with BMI 30 or higher to intensive, multicomponent behavioral interventions.

name: Depression screening
frequency: No specified interval; commonly annually where follow-up systems exist
test: Validated instrument such as PHQ-9, PHQ-2, or Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale
note: Recommends screening all adults 19 and older, including pregnant and postpartum persons and older adults, paired with systems for diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up.

name: Unhealthy alcohol use screening
screensFor: Risky drinking and alcohol use disorder
frequency: No specified interval; commonly at annual primary care visits
test: Validated brief instrument (SASQ, AUDIT-C, or full AUDIT); brief counseling for positive screens
note: Recommends screening all adults 18+, including pregnant persons, and providing brief behavioral counseling to reduce unhealthy alcohol use.

name: Tobacco use screening and cessation counseling
frequency: Ask about tobacco use at every visit
test: Ask all adults about tobacco use; advise users to quit and offer counseling plus FDA-approved cessation medication
note: Clinicians should ask all adults about tobacco use, advise users to stop, and provide behavioral interventions and FDA-approved pharmacotherapy.

name: HIV screening
frequency: At least once; more often if ongoing risk
test: HIV antigen/antibody immunoassay (4th-generation blood test)
note: Screen all adolescents and adults ages 15-65 at least once. Screen those outside that range who are at increased risk. Repeat testing for people at ongoing higher risk. CDC recommends all adults 13-64 be screened at least once.

name: Hepatitis C screening
frequency: One-time for most adults; periodic if ongoing risk
test: Anti-HCV antibody test; reflex to HCV RNA (PCR) if reactive
note: One-time screening for all adults 18-79. CDC also recommends screening during each pregnancy. Periodic testing for people with continued risk such as injection drug use.

name: Hepatitis B screening
frequency: At least once in a lifetime for all adults
test: Triple panel: HBsAg, anti-HBs, and total anti-HBc (single blood draw)
note: CDC (2023) recommends universal screening of all adults 18+ at least once with the triple panel. Pregnant women should be screened with HBsAg at the first prenatal visit each pregnancy.

name: Chlamydia screening (women)
frequency: Annually while sexually active and at risk
test: Nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) on vaginal swab or urine
note: Screen all sexually active women 24 and younger, and older women at increased risk (new or multiple partners, partner with an STI, inconsistent condom use).

name: Gonorrhea screening (women)
frequency: Annually while sexually active and at risk
test: Nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) on vaginal swab or urine
note: Screen all sexually active women 24 and younger, and older women at increased risk. USPSTF found insufficient evidence to screen men. CDC recommends at least annual screening at exposure sites for sexually active MSM.

name: Syphilis screening (at-risk adults)
frequency: Periodic; at least annually for those at continued risk
test: Treponemal or nontreponemal serologic test (e.g., RPR/VDRL with treponemal confirmation)
note: Screen adolescents and adults at increased risk: MSM, people with HIV, history of incarceration or commercial sex work, and those in high-prevalence areas. Given rising congenital syphilis, maintain a low threshold to screen.

name: Influenza (flu) vaccine
screensFor: Seasonal influenza infection and its complications
frequency: Annually (every flu season)
test: Age-appropriate inactivated, recombinant, or live attenuated influenza vaccine; one dose each season
note: Recommended for all adults annually, ideally by end of October. Adults 65+ should preferentially receive a higher-dose or adjuvanted product.

name: Tdap / Td booster
frequency: Every 10 years
test: One-time Tdap dose if not previously received, then Td or Tdap booster every 10 years
note: Adults who never received Tdap should get one dose to cover pertussis, then a booster every 10 years. Pregnant persons should receive Tdap during each pregnancy (weeks 27-36).

name: COVID-19 vaccine
frequency: Per current ACIP (shared clinical decision-making; annual updated dose)
test: Updated (current-season) mRNA or protein-subunit COVID-19 vaccine
note: For 2025-2026, ACIP recommends COVID-19 vaccination based on individual/shared clinical decision-making rather than a blanket universal recommendation. Risk-benefit is most favorable for those 65+ and those at increased risk for severe disease.

name: RSV vaccine
screensFor: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) lower respiratory disease
frequency: One-time (single dose)
test: Single dose of an RSV vaccine (Abrysvo, Arexvy, or mResvia)
note: Universally recommended as a single dose for all adults 75+ not previously vaccinated. Not currently recommended for revaccination. See the risk-based entry for adults 50-74 who qualify earlier.

name: RSV vaccine (increased-risk adults 50-74)
screensFor: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) lower respiratory disease
frequency: One-time (single dose)
test: Single dose of an RSV vaccine (Abrysvo, Arexvy, or mResvia)
note: Recommended as a single dose for adults 50-74 at increased risk of severe RSV: chronic lung, heart, kidney, or liver disease; diabetes; obesity; immunocompromise; neurologic conditions; frailty; or residence in long-term care.

name: Recombinant zoster (shingles) vaccine (Shingrix)
frequency: One-time 2-dose series
test: Recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix): 2 doses given 2-6 months apart
note: Recommended for all immunocompetent adults 50+ as a 2-dose series. Immunocompromised adults 19+ should also receive it, with a shortened interval permitted. Recommended even for those who previously received the older live zoster vaccine (Zostavax).

name: Pneumococcal vaccine
frequency: One-time (single conjugate dose for most)
test: A single dose of PCV20 or PCV21; or PCV15 followed by PPSV23
note: ACIP lowered the universal age from 65 to 50 (2025 schedule): all adults 50+ not previously vaccinated should get PCV20 or PCV21, or PCV15 followed by PPSV23. Adults 19-49 with certain chronic conditions or immunocompromise should also be vaccinated.

name: HPV vaccine
screensFor: Human papillomavirus infection and HPV-associated cancers/warts
frequency: One-time catch-up series through age 26
test: 9-valent HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9); 2- or 3-dose series depending on age at initiation
note: Routine catch-up recommended for everyone through age 26 if not adequately vaccinated. For adults 27-45, not routinely recommended but may be given based on shared clinical decision-making.

name: Hepatitis B vaccine
screensFor: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection and its complications
frequency: One-time series (2, 3, or 4 doses depending on product)
test: Hepatitis B vaccine series (2-dose Heplisav-B, or 3-dose Engerix-B/Recombivax HB)
note: Universal recommendation for all adults 19-59 who have not completed the series. Adults 60+ may be vaccinated at their discretion, and are specifically recommended if they have risk factors.

name: Kidney testing in diabetes (eGFR + urine ACR)
frequency: At least annually (more often once CKD is established)
test: eGFR (creatinine blood test) plus spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR)
note: Everyone with diabetes should be tested at least yearly. Start at diagnosis for type 2; start 5 years after diagnosis for type 1. If UACR is >=30 or eGFR <60, testing increases to 1-4 times per year. This is monitoring in an at-risk group, not an age-gated screen -- it guards against kidney failure and dialysis.

name: Kidney testing in hypertension (eGFR + urine ACR)
frequency: Annually
test: eGFR (creatinine blood test) plus spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR)
note: People with high blood pressure are an established at-risk group and should have both eGFR and urine ACR checked. Hypertension both causes and results from kidney disease. Confirm any abnormal result with a repeat test before diagnosing CKD.
